items.title: Andy Burnham to spend first working day at new No 10 North in Manchester – UK politics live
items.content_html:
PM will chair the inaugural meeting of the national economic council that will aim to shift economic decision-making away ‌from London

The Times has reported this morning that Andy Burnham is expected to “move quickly” to scale up North Sea oil and gas production as part of his policy blitz to tackle the cost of living.

Industry sources suggested to the newspaper that the prime minister and his new energy secretary, Miatta Fahnbulleh, are considering approving oil and gas production at the Jackdaw and Rosebank sites off the coast of Scotland to help drive down costs this winter.

In a joint report with Friends of the Earth in 2021, her organisation called for a total end to new drilling in the North Sea basin, the cancellation of all future licensing rounds, the revocation of all undeveloped licences and the rejection of any applications for development consent. Today, those who collaborated with her in the past are watching her first steps closely.

“She must hold the line against harmful fossil fuel expansion – including the highly contentious Jackdaw and Rosebank fields – make cheap, clean energy available to all and build on Ed Miliband’s efforts to scale up our renewable power capacity,” said Imogen Dow, energy lead for Friends of the Earth.”

The Trump administration unveiled the latest tranche of tariffs for countries over their “failure to impose and effectively enforce a prohibition on the importation of goods produced with forced labour”.

We absolutely need to see the strait of Hormuz be open and for freedom of navigation to happen there, for commercial shipping to be allowed through, and we very much call on Iran to allow that to be the case.

items.title: What to know about Trump’s new tariffs on more than 80 countries
items.content_html:
Tariffs ranging from 10%-12.5%, taking effect on Friday, are US president’s latest effort to circumvent Congress

Just before a temporary levy on global imports was set to expire, the Trump administration issued a fresh wave of tariffs on more than 80 countries late on Thursday, ranging from 10% to 12.5%. The new tariffs take effect Friday morning.

It’s Donald Trump’s latest effort to continue imposing global levies without congressional approval, despite the supreme court ruling in February that found the US president had illegally used his executive power to implement his global trade policies. Trump has long maintained that the US has been paying more than its fair share in tariffs, and that increased duties are necessary to boost American manufacturing and jobs.

items.title: Total solar eclipse books out hotels two years in advance in New Zealand city
items.content_html:
Dunedin will be the focal point, having last experienced such an eclipse in 1163 – before any humans reached New Zealand

Hotels are booking out two years in advance in the New Zealand city of Dunedin as it prepares for the region’s first total solar eclipse in more than 850 years.

At 4.17pm on 22 July 2028, the moon will pass in front of the sun, casting a roughly 100km-wide shadowy band across parts of southern New Zealand. Everywhere within that band – from tourist hotspot Queenstown in the west to Dunedin city in the east – will be plunged into daytime darkness for 2 minutes and 51 seconds.

items.title: Sperm whales change ‘vowel’ sounds when boats are nearby, scientists discover
items.content_html:
Researchers listening off the Caribbean island of Dominica found variations in the mammals’ audible clicks when marine traffic was close

Sperm whales produce a specific pattern of clicking noises when they hear the noise of boats, researchers have found. The pattern is so distinct that the scientists who identified it said they can now use it to predict when ships are nearby.

Researchers from Project Ceti (the Cetacean Translation Initiative), a non-profit research organisation working to decode sperm whale communication, spent four years tuning in on the calls of 15 sperm whales off the coast of Dominica.

items.title: Japan records its first ‘cruelly hot day’ as cities swelter in 40-degree heat
items.content_html:
In April Japan coined new term kokushobi for days above 40C as country advises workers to wear shorts to the office to cope with stifling heat

Japan logged its first official kokushobi or “cruelly hot day” on Tuesday since the new term for when temperatures top 40 degrees Celsius was introduced in April. The Japan Meteorological Agency coined the new designation to warn the public of the heat dangers of days that were once freak weather events but have now become more common.

The central Japan cities of Gujo and Tajimi in Gifu prefecture, along with Toyota in Aichi prefecture, all hit 40C. Gujo’s 40C and Toyota’s 40.1C were all-time highs recorded at their respective weather stations.

items.content_html: The US will impose a 25% tariff on most imports from Brazil starting later this month. It's the first action in the Trump administration's new tariff strategy, after the Supreme Court struck down its prior impositions.

items.title: AI: Why Europe is falling behind, and how it can catch up
items.content_html: Europe trails the US and China in artificial intelligence. Economist and Nobel laureate Philippe Aghion says better research funding, venture capital and AI innovation can help it catch up.

items.title: Will gold prices extend their record-breaking run?
items.content_html: Predictions suggest the price of the precious metal will keep hitting new record highs as investors seek a safe bet in turbulent times. Some forecasts say prices could double within five years.

items.title: East-West border death zone now Germany's biodiversity oasis
items.content_html: Many endangered species now live in the area that once formed the border between East and West Germany. Environment Minister Carsten Schneider recently visited the "Green Belt," and DW went along for the ride.

items.title: Could Russia be about to lose its grip on chess leadership?
items.content_html: Russians have long pulled the strings when it comes to the governing body of international chess. But now two Germans are vying for FIDE's top job.

items.title: German Chancellor Friedrich Merz makes appeal to AfD voters
items.content_html: Germany's chancellor has insisted that his coalition has "regained its footing" — despite dismal poll numbers. Friedrich Merz also cautioned against voting for the far-right AfD party.

items.title: England vs. Argentina: A football rivalry full of history
items.content_html: From the Falkland Islands to Diego Maradona and David Beckham, there is so much history between the two 2026 World Cup semifinalists.

items.title: Running dry: How to store more groundwater for dry seasons
items.content_html: More frequent weather extremes are leading to water shortages during droughts and flooding during heavy rain. But groundwater storage can help secure water for dry seasons while mitigating extreme rain.

items.title: Why Indonesia's economy is under pressure
items.content_html: Southeast Asia's largest economy risks losing its emerging-market status as President Prabowo Subianto's spending plans rattle markets. Can he deliver growth?

items.title: 150 years of the Bayreuth Festival: Confronting a troubled legacy
items.content_html: Should the past be confronted — or avoided? Why the Wagner festival's handling of its Nazi-era legacy remains a contentious issue in its 150th anniversary year.

items.title: Germany's Merz receives death threat from Iranian newspaper
items.content_html: In retaliation for Khamenei's death, a Tehran daily listed 13 world leaders, including German Chancellor Friedrich Merz, as targets. Politicians voiced concern.

items.title: T. rex 'Gus' sells for record-breaking $50.1 million
items.content_html: The Tyrannosaurus rex skeleton surpassed its pre-sale estimate and set a new auction record for a dinosaur fossil.

items.title: Micronutrients are good, a balanced diet is better
items.content_html: Chronic illnesses, heart disease, obesity ― we know eating too much of the wrong foods can be unhealthy. But what about "good" foods: Is modern farming diluting micronutrients in our fruit and vegetables?

items.title: Germany's Ahr Valley: Rebuilding better after deadly flood
items.content_html: New walls, redesigned bridges and restored floodplains: Towns in a small German valley are testing how they can survive the next extreme flood.

items.title: Le cercle marocain d'Ali Lmrabet
items.content_html: Le journaliste marocain Ali Lmrabet, dans une capture d'image d'une vidéo sur sa chaîne YouTube.

items.title: Germany set to restrict its Freedom of Information Act
items.content_html: Critics fear the reforms could weaken government transparency by curbing civil society's access to official information and limiting requests from foreigners.

items.title: World Cup: Balogun red and expansion keep FIFA in spotlight
items.content_html: The fallout from Folarin Balogun's red card reversal continues as reports suggest the chairman of FIFA’s disciplinary committee made the decision unilaterally. Meanwhile, the World Cup could be set to expand once more.

items.title: Are German companies leaving the country?
items.content_html: Should I stay or should I go? Given Germany's high cost and sluggish growth, some business leaders are mulling relocating, or at least investing abroad. DW has a look at the numbers.

items.title: California's largest lake is turning to dust
items.content_html: The Salton Sea is drying up, worsening air pollution for nearby communities. Officials are racing to contain it, but for some families, it's already too late.

items.title: Germany's Ruhr Valley: Cities on brink of financial collapse
items.content_html: With tax revenues down and social spending up, more and more cities and towns in the Ruhrgebiet are struggling. Oberhausen is particularly deep in the red.

items.title: Germany news: Neuschwanstein killer fights extradition to US
items.content_html: A man who murdered a woman at Neuschwanstein Castle says he fears execution in the US. Germany has been hit by another heat wave, though no records are likely to be broken this time, forecasters say.

items.title: How a Jewish heir is taking on Germany's far-right AfD
items.content_html: The AfD is using the iconic GDR-era Simson moped in its campaign. Now, a Jewish heir to the founding family is fighting to protect the company's legacy.

items.title: La Russie est en train de perdre la guerre, l’Ukraine ne la gagne qu’à moitié
items.content_html: Un soldat et une femme s'embrassent à Kiev, la capitale de l'Ukraine, le 19 février 2023, quelques jours seulement avant le premier anniversaire de l'invasion russe.

items.title: Poutine remplace Zelensky comme perdant aux yeux de Trump
items.content_html: Donald Trump, mercredi au sommet de l'Otan à Ankara.

items.title: Heat is especially hard on people taking antidepressants
items.content_html: High temperatures can increase the risk of dizziness, dehydration, exhaustion and other heat-related problems for people taking antidepressants.

items.title: Germany's richest man takes on Big Tech
items.content_html: Supermarket billionaire Dieter Schwarz is investing billions in his cloud company, Schwarz Digits, to rival Google, Microsoft and Amazon.

items.title: Ukraine's Bidnyi 'outraged' by Russia's Olympic return
items.content_html: In exclusive comments to DW, Ukraine's sports minister, Matviy Bidnyi, said he is outraged by the IOC opening the door to Russia's return to the Olympics

items.title: When London stank to high heaven
items.content_html: In 1858, London sweltered through an unbearable heat wave. The River Thames reeked so badly it left people gasping for air. "The Great Stink" went down in history — and ultimately gave London a modern sewer system.

items.title: Vegan pregnancy tests and other eco wins this week
items.content_html: Keeping cool in the heat with a millennia-old tradition in India, a German company making animal-free medical tests, and building from bottles in Mozambique.

items.title: Why the World Cup can be good for your mental health
items.content_html: The World Cup can offer more than just entertainment, it can also benefit your mental well-being. DW explains why.

items.title: What's behind Germany's World Cup woes?
items.content_html: Germany have endured three straight disappointing World Cups. Experts say the reasons why are connected to football, but also to German society.

items.title: What happens when your fun-loving grandfather — a good husband, an upright citizen — turns out to have been a Nazi?
items.content_html: Since millions of NSDAP membership cards have been made available online, many Germans have been surprised to discover that their ancestors weren't always as innocent as family stories had led them to believe.

items.title: Bonnie Tyler, singer of 'Total Eclipse of the Heart,' dies at 75
items.content_html: The Welsh singer was best known for the 1980s rock opera hit, along with other songs, including "It's a Heartache."

items.title: Is the Strait of Hormuz still Iran's trump card?
items.content_html: Iran's latest attacks show it can easily disrupt shipping, energy markets and draw in Gulf neighbors. DW asks how much leverage Tehran really has over Washington and if its high-risk strategy could backfire.

items.title: Egypt's wrath after World Cup exit: 'The match was rigged'
items.content_html: Egypt were left seething by their narrow and dramatic round-of-16 defeat against Argentina. Coach Hossam Hassan accused the referee of favoring the defending World Cup champions.

items.title: Assassin's Creed: Why pop culture is hooked to pirates
items.content_html: Pirates have captured our imagination for centuries. With "Assassin's Creed Black Flag Resynced," gamers can now head off on another pirate adventure. How has our concept of pirates evolved with pop culture?

items.title: The Australian tree supercharging global wildfires
items.content_html: As Europe experiences another summer of extreme heat and wildfire warnings, one tree imported from Australia is coming under renewed scrutiny across the world: eucalyptus.

items.title: Brain tumors: New vaccine offers patients hope for more time
items.content_html: A new therapy offers people with seemingly incurable brain tumors scope for hope: It could slow the progression of the disease and extend patients' lives.

items.title: Conflict, climate change, cash-strapped: Why poverty persists
items.content_html: With just under five years left to complete the UN's Sustainable Development Goals, a new report shows what's hampering progress. And what the solutions are.

items.title: Heat-resistant housing: How we need to adapt our homes
items.content_html: In Germany, it is time to rethink and rebuild. Many apartments were not designed for heat waves. Fortunately, there are ways to adapt existing buildings to the changing climate — but air conditioning isn't the best.

items.title: Mbappe hits back at racist insults from Paraguay senator
items.content_html: A Paraguayan lawmaker has sparked outrage with racist insults directed at footballer Kylian Mbappe. The French FA says it will take legal action, and President Emmanuel Macron has expressed support for the player.

items.title: Could football break away from FIFA and its World Cup?
items.content_html: FIFA’s actions at the World Cup, and closeness to Donald Trump, have put the organization under fire. Federations, fans, coaches and players have gone public with complaints, but is there a way out?

items.title: Why Volkswagen's huge workforce became a costly burden
items.content_html: The struggling German carmaker plans to cut up to 100,000 jobs from its 630,000-strong workforce. How did VW's headcount get so bloated, and will the cuts be enough to survive the Chinese EV onslaught?

items.title: World Cup 2026: Trump, FIFA and the Balogun controversy
items.content_html: One of the few certainties of World Cup football has been rocked after Donald Trump lobbied FIFA to overturn a US red card suspension. The decision has caused outrage but it isn't completely without precedent.

items.title: Fashion against fascism: When trademarks deter neo-Nazis
items.content_html: In Germany, where Nazi symbols are banned, activists are using trademark law to fight far-right merchandise.

items.title: Chinese Tianwen-2 space probe reaches asteroid for sampling
items.content_html: After a yearlong flight of a billion kilometers, a Chinese space probe is set to take samples from an asteroid. The mission is part of what President Xi Jinping calls the country's "space dream."

items.title: European space telescope Euclid spots oldest quasars yet
items.content_html: Think of quasars as time machines. Rare but revealing. They are light from the beginnings of the universe that let astronomers investigate how early galaxies formed.

items.title: Peut-être que les vies américaines auront une seconde chance.
items.content_html: Gregory Peck et Brock Peters dans "To Kill a Mockingbird", une adaptation du roman de Harper Lee, symbole du mouvement des droits civiques.

items.title: Why US train travel lags behind Asia and Europe
items.content_html: As flights get pricier and gas prices stay high, more US travelers are turning to trains — and hitting a system built for freight, not people.

items.title: Free electricity in Australia, and the week's other eco wins
items.content_html: Germans save an ancient woodland from coal mining, Australia gives away surplus solar power, and how one city became the world's best place for cyclists?

items.title: Far-right AfD threatens eastern Germany's energy transition
items.content_html: Germany's far-right AfD wants to revive coal and nuclear power and curb non-EU immigration. Businesses in eastern Germany warn that its policies could harm the economy.

items.title: Sick leave: Germany rising but not the worst in Europe
items.content_html: Sick leave in Germany has hit a new record, with workers taking an average of nearly 20 days off per year. As Chancellor Merz cracks down, how does the country's absenteeism compare to other countries?

items.title: The downfall: Julian Nagelsmann out as Germany coach
items.content_html: The German Football Associationand national team coach Nagelsmann have parted ways after Germany's poor display at the 2026 World Cup.

items.title: As heat waves loom, scientists wonder how humans will adapt
items.content_html: Temperatures are set to soar in the US around the July 4 holiday, a week after Europe struck new records. With experts predicting more intense and more frequent heat waves in the future, can our bodies adapt?

items.title: Is Mexico the 2026 World Cup's best host country?
items.content_html: Mexico has fully embraced its role as a World Cup host. Visitors are raving about the atmosphere and say they wish that there were more knockout-stage matches in Mexico as opposed to in the United States.

items.title: Fact check: Racist German fan petition story false
items.content_html: It has been claimed that following Germany's World Cup exit, some fans launched a petition seeking to ban "African" and "Muslim" players from the national team — and that DW reported the story. This is not true.

items.title: US declines to renew USMCA trade pact with Mexico, Canada
items.content_html: Lengthy negotiations are expected as the Trump administration seeks changes to a trade deal with Mexico and Canada that has streamlined supply chains across North America.

items.title: Bundesliga: Kathleen Krüger takes over as Hamburg's head of sport
items.content_html: Hamburg have presented Kathleen Krüger as the club's new board member responsible for sport. She is the first woman to fill such a role at a Bundesliga club on a permanent basis.

items.title: Digital euro: The plan to Trump-proof the EU's economies
items.content_html: The EU’s central bank is preparing a digital euro to cut dependence on Visa, Mastercard and Apple Pay. Success hinges on giving consumers an easy-to-use payment system without prompting a flight from bank savings.

items.title: Germany saves forest from mining, US opens land to drilling
items.content_html: Germans have won a fight to keep a coal mine out of an ancient forest. In the US, the Trump administration is opening protected lands for drilling and development.

items.title: Portugal's 'golden visa' delays spark legal fight
items.content_html: After waiting years for their "golden visas," investors are taking Portugal to court. Tougher citizenship rules have added fuel to the growing dispute.

items.title: Art biennial Manifesta revives Germany's postwar churches
items.content_html: Following the destruction of World War II, around 1,000 new churches were built in Germany's Ruhr region. Today, many stand empty, but the contemporary art show Manifesta is giving them a new lease on life.

items.title: Comedy legend Mel Brooks turns 100
items.content_html: A parody of Adolf Hitler in the landmark 1968 movie "The Producers" was just the beginning for US actor Mel Brooks. The 100-year-old comedian is still on a roll today.

items.title: Creative climate messaging and other eco wins of the week
items.content_html: An artist in Mali and climate scientists in the US are finding new ways to inform the public about the climate crisis.

items.title: Wagner festival reinstates Holocaust memorial event after cancellation
items.content_html: The Bayreuth Festival had canceled a planned memorial lecture featuring Jewish author and broadcaster Michel Friedman, drawing sharp criticism.

items.title: Bayer: Why so many Roundup weedkiller lawsuits?
items.content_html: Bayer was sued for failing to add cancer warnings to its controversial pesticide Roundup when US regulators opposed adding one. DW asks if the latest Supreme Court win will shield the chemical giant from paying billions.

items.title: Here's what southern Europeans know about surviving heat waves
items.content_html: People in countries like Greece, Italy and Spain have long lived with high temperatures. Here's what more northerly countries can learn from them about managing heat waves as they become more common.

items.title: Human rights expert Philippe Sands wins German Peace Prize
items.content_html: From advocating for the law of ecocide to representing Palestinian rights, the descendant of Holocaust survivors tirelessly fights against genocide and crimes against humanity.

items.title: How countries live with earthquakes and build resilience
items.content_html: Earthquakes are a fact of nature, but living with earthquakes doesn't have to mean living in fear. DW takes a closer look at some countries showing how preparation, innovation and determination can save lives.

items.title: Why Germans don't have air conditioning
items.content_html: Unlike the US or parts of Asia, many homes in Germany and northern Europe aren't equipped to deal with extreme heat. But as the world gets hotter, trends are changing.
